title: Calculating the test statistic in a z test for a proportion
description: Practice using sample data to calculate the test statistic in a one-sample z test for a proportion.
topic_path: Calculating the test statistic in a z test for a proportion

assessment_items.question_content:
The mayor of a town saw an article that claimed the national unemployment rate is $8\%$. They wondered if this held true in their town, so they took a sample of $200$ residents to test $H_0:p=0.08$ versus $H_\text{a}:p \neq 0.08$, where $p$ is the proportion of residents in the town that are unemployed. The sample included $22$ residents who were unemployed.

**Assuming that the conditions for inference have been met, identify the correct test statistic for this significance test.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample z-test for a proportion, our test statistic is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{statistic}-\text{parameter}}{\text{standard deviation of statistic}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\hat p$ is the sample proportion, the parameter $p_0$ is the proportion from the null hypothesis, and $n$ is the sample size.

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion of residents that were unemployed:

$\hat p=\dfrac{22}{200}=\blueD{0.11}$

Our parameter is the proportion from the null hypothesis, so we use $p_0=\greenD{0.08}$.

They took a sample of $200$ residents, so we use $n=\purpleD{200}$.

We plug those values into the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{0.11}-\greenD{0.08}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\greenD{0.08}(1-\greenD{0.08})}{\purpleD{200}}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{0.11-0.08}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.08(0.92)}{200}}}
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A campaign manager for a political candidate released a series of advertisements criticizing the opposing candidate in an upcoming election. The opposing candidate previously had the support of $45\%$ of voters, so the manager wants to test $H_0:p=0.45$ versus $H_\text{a}:p < 0.45$, where $p$ is the proportion of voters that support the opposing candidate.

After running the advertisements, the campaign manager obtained a random sample of $500$ voters and found that $200$ of those sampled supported the opposing candidate.

**Assuming that the conditions for inference have been met, identify the correct test statistic for this significance test.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample z-test for a proportion, our test statistic is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{statistic}-\text{parameter}}{\text{standard deviation of statistic}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\hat p$ is the sample proportion, the parameter $p_0$ is the proportion from the null hypothesis, and $n$ is the sample size.

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion of voters that support the opposing candidate:

$\hat p=\dfrac{200}{500}=\blueD{0.40}$

Our parameter is the proportion from the null hypothesis, so we use $p_0=\greenD{0.45}$.

They took a sample of $500$ voters, so we use $n=\purpleD{500}$.

We plug those values into the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{0.40}-\greenD{0.45}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\greenD{0.45}(1-\greenD{0.45})}{\purpleD{500}}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{0.40-0.45}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.45(0.55)}{500}}}
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A business knows that $30\%$ of its customers are less than $25$ years old. The business wants to increase this percentage, so they create a marketing campaign that targets this age group. After the campaign, they take a random sample of $50$ customers to test $H_0:p=0.30$ versus $H_\text{a}:p > 0.30$, where $p$ is the proportion of this business's customers who are less than $25$ years old after the marketing campaign.

The sample results show $18$ customers who are less $25$ years old.

**Assuming that the conditions for inference have been met, identify the correct test statistic for this significance test.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample z-test for a proportion, our test statistic is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{statistic}-\text{parameter}}{\text{standard deviation of statistic}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\hat p$ is the sample proportion, the parameter $p_0$ is the proportion from the null hypothesis, and $n$ is the sample size.

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion of customers who are less than $25$ years old:

$\hat p=\dfrac{18}{50}=\blueD{0.36}$

Our parameter is the proportion from the null hypothesis, so we use $p_0=\greenD{0.30}$.

They took a sample of $50$ customers, so we use $n=\purpleD{50}$.

We plug those values into the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{0.36}-\greenD{0.30}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\greenD{0.30}(1-\greenD{0.30})}{\purpleD{50}}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{0.36-0.30}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.30(0.70)}{50}}}
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Amanda read a report saying that $49\%$ of teachers in the United States were members of a labor union. She wanted to test whether this was true in her state, so she took a random sample of $300$ teachers from her state to test $H_0:p=0.49$ versus $H_\text{a}:p \neq 0.49$, where $p$ is the proportion of teachers in her state who are members of a labor union.

The sample results showed $180$ teachers were members of a labor union.

**Assuming that the conditions for inference have been met, identify the correct test statistic for Amanda's significance test.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample z-test for a proportion, our test statistic is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{statistic}-\text{parameter}}{\text{standard deviation of statistic}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\hat p$ is the sample proportion, the parameter $p_0$ is the proportion from the null hypothesis, and $n$ is the sample size.

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion of teachers who members of a labor union:

$\hat p=\dfrac{180}{300}=\blueD{0.60}$

Our parameter is the proportion from the null hypothesis, so we use $p_0=\greenD{0.49}$.

Amanda took a sample of $300$ teachers, so we use $n=\purpleD{300}$.

We plug those values into the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{0.60}-\greenD{0.49}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\greenD{0.49}(1-\greenD{0.49})}{\purpleD{300}}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{0.60-0.49}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.49(0.51)}{300}}}
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Elliot read a report from a previous year saying that $6\%$ of adults in his city biked to work. He wanted to test whether this had changed, so he took a random sample of $240$ adults in his city to test $H_0:p=0.06$ versus $H_\text{a}:p \neq 0.06$, where $p$ is the proportion of adults in Elliot's city that bike to work.

The sample results showed $24$ adults who biked to work.

**Assuming that the conditions for inference have been met, identify the correct test statistic for Elliot's significance test.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample z-test for a proportion, our test statistic is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{statistic}-\text{parameter}}{\text{standard deviation of statistic}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\hat p$ is the sample proportion, the parameter $p_0$ is the proportion from the null hypothesis, and $n$ is the sample size.

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion of adults who bike to work:

$\hat p=\dfrac{24}{240}=\blueD{0.1}$

Our parameter is the proportion from the null hypothesis, so we use $p_0=\greenD{0.06}$.

Elliot took a sample of $240$ adults, so we use $n=\purpleD{240}$.

We plug those values into the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{0.1}-\greenD{0.06}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\greenD{0.06}(1-\greenD{0.06})}{\purpleD{240}}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{0.1-0.06}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.06(0.94)}{240}}}
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A large poll showed that $42\%$ of adults approved of their nation's prime minister. Margot wanted to test if it had decreased, so she took a random sample of $500$ adults in that nation and found that $160$ of those them approved of the prime minister.

She wants to test $H_0:p=0.42$ versus $H_\text{a}:p < 0.42$, were $p$ is the proportion of adults in this nation who approve of the prime minister.

**Assuming that the conditions for inference have been met, identify the correct test statistic for Margot's significance test.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample z-test for a proportion, our test statistic is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{statistic}-\text{parameter}}{\text{standard deviation of statistic}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\hat p$ is the sample proportion, the parameter $p_0$ is the proportion from the null hypothesis, and $n$ is the sample size.

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion of adults who approve of the prime minister:

$\hat p=\dfrac{160}{500}=\blueD{0.32}$

Our parameter is the proportion from the null hypothesis, so we use $p_0=\greenD{0.42}$.

Margot took a sample of $500$ adults, so we use $n=\purpleD{500}$.

We plug those values into the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{0.32}-\greenD{0.42}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\greenD{0.42}(1-\greenD{0.42})}{\purpleD{500}}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{0.32-0.42}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.42(0.58)}{500}}}
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Jake heard that spinning—rather than flipping—a penny raises the probability above $50\%$ that the penny lands showing heads. He tests this by spinning $10$ different pennies $10$ times each, so he's willing to treat these spins as a random sample. In his $100$ spins, the penny landed showing "heads" in $68$ spins. He wants to use these results to test $H_0: p=0.50$ versus $H_\text{a}: p>0.50$, where $p$ is the true proportion of spins that a penny would land showing "heads".

**Assuming that the conditions for inference have been met, calculate the test statistic for Jake's significance test.**  
*You may round to two decimal places.*

$z=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample z-test for a proportion, our test statistic is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{statistic}-\text{parameter}}{\text{standard deviation of statistic}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\hat p$ is the sample proportion, the parameter $p_0$ is the proportion from the null hypothesis, and $n$ is the sample size.

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion of spins that landed showing heads:

$\hat p=\dfrac{68}{100}=\blueD{0.68}$

Our parameter is the proportion from the null hypothesis, so we use $p_0=\greenD{0.50}$.

Since he has a sample of $100$ spins, we use $n=\purpleD{100}$.

We plug those values into the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{0.68}-\greenD{0.50}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\greenD{0.50}(1-\greenD{0.50})}{\purpleD{100}}}}
\end{align}$

###Answer

$z=3.6$

assessment_items.question_content:
According to a very large poll in 2015, about $90\%$ of homes in California had access to the internet. Market researchers want to test if that proportion is now higher, so they take a random sample of $100$ homes in California and find that $96$ of them have access to the internet.

The researchers will test $H_0: p=0.90$ versus $H_\text{a}: p>0.90$, where $p$ is the proportion of homes in California that have access to the internet.

**Assuming that the conditions for inference have been met, calculate the test statistic for their significance test.**  
*You may round to two decimal places.*

$z=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample z-test for a proportion, our test statistic is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{statistic}-\text{parameter}}{\text{standard deviation of statistic}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\hat p$ is the sample proportion, the parameter $p_0$ is the proportion from the null hypothesis, and $n$ is the sample size.

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion of homes that had access to the internet:

$\hat p=\dfrac{96}{100}=\blueD{0.96}$

Our parameter is the proportion from the null hypothesis, so we use $p_0=\greenD{0.90}$.

Since there were $100$ homes in the sample, we use $n=\purpleD{100}$.

We plug those values into the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{0.96}-\greenD{0.90}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\greenD{0.90}(1-\greenD{0.90})}{\purpleD{100}}}}
\end{align}$

###Answer

$z=2$

assessment_items.question_content:
Ellie read an article claiming that $10\%$ of people in her county were senior citizens, and she thought that it may be higher for residents in her city. She took a random sample of $225$ people from her city and found that $36$ of them were senior citizens.

She wants to test $H_0:p=0.10$ versus $H_\text{a}:p > 0.10$, where $p$ is the proportion of people in her city that are senior citizens.

**Assuming that the conditions for inference have been met, calculate the test statistic for Ellie's significance test.**  
*You may round to two decimal places.*

$z=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample z-test for a proportion, our test statistic is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{statistic}-\text{parameter}}{\text{standard deviation of statistic}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\hat p$ is the sample proportion, the parameter $p_0$ is the proportion from the null hypothesis, and $n$ is the sample size.

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion of people that were senior citizens:

$\hat p=\dfrac{36}{225}=\blueD{0.16}$

Our parameter is the proportion from the null hypothesis, so we use $p_0=\greenD{0.10}$.

Since there were $225$ people in the sample, we use $n=\purpleD{225}$.

We plug those values into the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{0.16}-\greenD{0.10}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\greenD{0.10}(1-\greenD{0.10})}{\purpleD{225}}}}
\end{align}$

###Answer

$z=3$

assessment_items.question_content:
A professor gives a multiple choice exam where each question has five choices. The professor decides not to count a question against students if the class as a whole does significantly worse on the question than they would have done simply by guessing. In other words, the teacher tests $H_0: p=0.20$ versus $H_\text{a}: p<0.20$ for each question, where $p$ is the proportion of students who would correctly answer the question.

Suppose that $14$ of $100$ students correctly answer a particular question.

**Assuming that the conditions for inference have been met, calculate the test statistic for the professor's significance test.**  
*You may round to two decimal places.*

$z=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample z-test for a proportion, our test statistic is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{statistic}-\text{parameter}}{\text{standard deviation of statistic}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\hat p$ is the sample proportion, the parameter $p_0$ is the proportion from the null hypothesis, and $n$ is the sample size.

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the proportion of students that correctly answered the question:

$\hat p=\dfrac{14}{100}=\blueD{0.14}$

Our parameter is the proportion from the null hypothesis, so we use $p_0=\greenD{0.20}$.

Since we there are $100$ students, we use $n=\purpleD{100}$.

We plug those values into the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{0.14}-\greenD{0.20}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\greenD{0.20}(1-\greenD{0.20})}{\purpleD{100}}}}
\end{align}$

###Answer

$z=-1.5$

assessment_items.question_content:
According to a large poll in a previous year, about $80\%$ of homes in a certain county had access to high-speed internet. Market researchers wondered if that proportion had changed, so they took a random sample of $64$ homes from that county and found that $48$ of them had access to high-speed internet.

They want to use this sample data to test $H_0:p=0.8$ versus $H_\text{a}:p\neq0.8$, where $p$ is the proportion of homes in this county with high-speed internet access.

**Assuming that the conditions for inference have been met, calculate the test statistic for their significance test.**  
*You may round to two decimal places.*

$z=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample z-test for a proportion, our test statistic is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{statistic}-\text{parameter}}{\text{standard deviation of statistic}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\hat p$ is the sample proportion, the parameter $p_0$ is the proportion from the null hypothesis, and $n$ is the sample size.

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion of homes with access to high-speed internet:

$\hat p=\dfrac{48}{64}=\blueD{0.75}$

Our parameter is the proportion from the null hypothesis, so we use $p_0=\greenD{0.80}$.

Since we there are $64$ homes in the sample, we use $n=\purpleD{64}$.

We plug those values into the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{0.75}-\greenD{0.80}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\greenD{0.80}(1-\greenD{0.80})}{\purpleD{64}}}}
\end{align}$

###Answer

$z=-1$

assessment_items.question_content:
Abdullah is a quality control expert at a factory that paints car parts. He knows that $20\%$ of parts have an error in their painting. He recommended a change in the painting process, and he wanted to see if this error rate had changed. He took a random sample of $400$ parts painted in a month and found that $60$ had an error.

He wants to test $H_0:p=0.2$ versus $H_\text{a}:p \neq 0.2$, where $p$ is the proportion of all parts with the error in their painting that month.

**Assuming that the conditions for inference have been met, calculate the test statistic for Abdullah's significance test.**  
*You may round to two decimal places.*

$z=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample z-test for a proportion, our test statistic is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{statistic}-\text{parameter}}{\text{standard deviation of statistic}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\hat p$ is the sample proportion, the parameter $p_0$ is the proportion from the null hypothesis, and $n$ is the sample size.

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion of parts with an error:

$\hat p=\dfrac{60}{400}=\blueD{0.15}$

Our parameter is the proportion from the null hypothesis, so we use $p_0=\greenD{0.20}$.

Since we there are $400$ parts in the sample, we use $n=\purpleD{400}$.

We plug those values into the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{0.15}-\greenD{0.20}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\greenD{0.20}(1-\greenD{0.20})}{\purpleD{400}}}}
\end{align}$

###Answer

$z=-2.5$
